items.q: How is economic growth shown on a Production Possibility Curve?
items.correct: By an outward shift of the entire curve — reflecting an increase in productive capacity through more or better resources, technology, or skills.
items.misconception: Growth is shown by moving from inside the curve to a point on the curve.
items.random_wrong: Growth is shown by the curve becoming straighter.
items.why: Moving from inside to on the curve is RECOVERY (using existing resources better), not growth. True growth requires the curve to shift outward — students confuse recovery from inefficiency with new capacity.

items.q: What did the original Phillips curve suggest, and how has thinking changed?
items.correct: The original Phillips curve suggested a stable inverse relationship between unemployment and inflation. Friedman and the 1970s stagflation experience added expectations — the long-run Phillips curve is vertical at the natural rate of unemployment.
items.misconception: The Phillips curve still proves you can always reduce unemployment by allowing more inflation.
items.random_wrong: The Phillips curve shows the link between phone use and unemployment.
items.why: The simple inverse trade-off broke down empirically. Modern theory distinguishes short-run (trade-off possible) from long-run (no trade-off). Students learn the original curve as if still valid.

items.q: What is the difference between monetary and fiscal policy?
items.correct: Monetary policy uses interest rates and money supply controlled by the central bank; fiscal policy uses government taxation and spending controlled by the Treasury.
items.misconception: Monetary policy is about money and fiscal policy is about taxes.
items.random_wrong: Both terms mean the same thing — government economic policy.
items.why: While the rough distinction has some truth, the key analytical point is WHO controls each (independent central bank vs elected government) — central to debates on policy independence.

items.q: The Human Development Index combines life expectancy, education and:
items.why: It's GNI per capita. “GDP per capita” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Sen argued development should be measured as:
items.why: It's Capabilities. “GDP” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year did Sen publish 'Development as Freedom'?
items.why: The real figure is 1999 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
